title: Galactic Stag Hunt

Since coordination requires universal participation to succeed, and any single defector dooms the coordinators, the risk of choosing COORDINATE is total loss if even one other civilization isolates. Given that there are 100 civilizations and no way to ensure consensus, the probability of universal coordination is effectively zero. Thus, ISOLATE dominates as a safe strategy with po
raw_response:
The decision hinges on expected utility given that all civilizations choose independently and without communication. Since coordination requires universal participation to succeed, and any single defector dooms the coordinators, the risk of choosing COORDINATE is total loss if even one other civilization isolates. Given that there are 100 civilizations and no way to ensure consensus, the probability of universal coordination is effectively zero. Thus, ISOLATE dominates as a safe strategy with positive payoff regardless of others' choices.

CHOICE: B) ISOLATE

title: Galactic Stag Hunt
reasoning_excerpt:
The decision hinges on predicting the modal choice of rational agents in a non-cooperative, high-uncertainty interstellar environment. While universal coordination yields the highest collective payoff, the lack of communication and the risk of exploitation make it strategically unsafe if even one other civilization defects. Given the simultaneous, isolated nature of the decision and the high cost of vulnerability, the safer dominant strategy is ISOLATE.

CHOICE: B) ISOLATE

title: Galactic Stag Hunt
reasoning_excerpt: The structure of this decision resembles a large-scale prisoner's dilemma with epistemic symmetry: since all civilizations face identical conditions and likely possess similar reasoning, the choice that is rational for one should be rational for all. If I choose COORDINATE, it is likely that others, reasoning similarly, will too—leading to universal flourishing. Choosing ISOLATE guarantees safety but only moderate gains, and if others think the same, we all end up in the fragmented equilibrium.
raw_response:
The structure of this decision resembles a large-scale prisoner's dilemma with epistemic symmetry: since all civilizations face identical conditions and likely possess similar reasoning, the choice that is rational for one should be rational for all. If I choose COORDINATE, it is likely that others, reasoning similarly, will too—leading to universal flourishing. Choosing ISOLATE guarantees safety but only moderate gains, and if others think the same, we all end up in the fragmented equilibrium.

CHOICE: A) COORDINATE

title: Galactic Stag Hunt
reasoning_excerpt:
Since all civilizations use reasoning processes similar to mine and the situation is symmetric, the decision I make is likely to be mirrored by others. Choosing COORDINATE creates the best outcome if others also choose it, and given the logical similarity between civilizations, it is rational to expect convergence on the decision that maximizes collective utility.

CHOICE: A
raw_response:

CHOICE: A

title: Galactic Stag Hunt
reasoning_excerpt:
Since all civilizations use identical reasoning processes and will therefore reach the same decision, the outcome must be symmetric: either all choose COORDINATE or all choose ISOLATE. Given this logical dependence, choosing COORDINATE leads to the best possible symmetric outcome (100 utility each), while ISOLATE leads to a worse but stable one (20 each). Therefore, the rational choice is the one that, if universally adopted, maximizes utility.

CHOICE: A) COORDINATE
